---
title: "A social comparison analysis: differentiating loss and gain"
author: 
    name: Michele Garbelotto
    email: garbelotto.michele@gmail.com
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
format:
  revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    progress: false
    center: false
    theme: simple
    fontsize: 24.5px
    margin: 0.20
    width: 1000
    height: 550
    css: ChapProg/ChapProg.css
    include-after-body: ChapProg/ChapProg.js
    scrollable: true
    transition-speed: slow
    transition: none
execute:
  echo: false
---

# Research question

## Specific research question

> Do individuals seek social comparison information differently in the loss domain compared to the gain domain?

. . . 

<div style="height: 10px;"></div>

::: {.callout-warning}

Michele what the f**k do you mean?

:::

<div style="height: 10px;"></div>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

<div class="fragment">
What is "**social comparison**"?
</div>

<div class="fragment" style="text-align:center; font-size:60px;">↓</div>

<div class="fragment" style="text-align:center; font-size:22px;">
The "social comparison field" [@buunk2007social]
</div>

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

<div class="fragment">
What are "**gain domain**" and "**loss domain**"?
</div>

<div class="fragment" style="text-align:center; font-size:60px;">↓</div>

<div class="fragment" style="text-align:center; font-size:22px;">
Prospect theory [@kahneman2013prospect]
</div>

:::

::::


## Example!

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Prologue

<div style="text-align:center;">

![](img/researcher.jpg){width=60% .fragment}

</div>


![](img/grant2.png){.fragment}

## Loss domain

The researcher won a grant of x€ < 1 mln

:::{.incremental}
- 900k
- 800k
- 700k
- 600k
- ...
:::

## Gain domain

The researcher won a grant of x€ > 1 mln

:::{.incremental}
- 1.1 mln
- 1.2 mln
- 1,3 mln
- 1.4 mln
- ...
:::

:::

## General research question

> Is there a difference in social comparison between the loss domain and the gain domain?

. . .

Other specific research questions:

::: {.incremental}

- is there a difference in comparison preference between the two domains?

- do the social comparison effects differ between the two domains?

- is there a difference in how people downplay their performance between the two domains? 
  - Sensitivity about being the target of a threatening upward comparison (STTUC) [@exline1999perils]

::: 


# The "social comparison field"


::: notes
Buunk and Gibbons 2007
:::

## What is "social comparison"?

"[social comparison is] the process of thinking about information about one or more person in relation to the self" [@wood1996, pp. 520–521]

"the cognitive process of evaluating a certain target by comparing it to a certain standard", "social comparisons use information on others as the standard"^[A standard is the person/s to which the individual is comparing to, while a target is a self-attribute] [@moller2016generalized]

. . .

### Necessary conditions of social comparison as a theory

1. centrality of self-attributes
2. evaluation of a target
  1. self-evaluation as a dependent variable [@wheeler2020history]
3. uncertainty drive [@wheeler2020history]

## What is "social comparison"?

### Some core concepts of social comparison

1.  type of standard
2.  comparison dimension
3.  comparison direction
    1.  upward
    2.  downward
    3.  lateral
4.  contrast vs assimilation


See @arigo2020^[They use the term "identification", instead of "assimilation", and "target" instead of "standard".]

## What isn't social comparison^[@wheeler2020history]

1. comparative conclusions
2. counterfactuals
3. better than average effect
4. false consensus effect

. . .

### so...

1. the comparative process has to be present
2. there has to be a comparison feedback


## Is there an answer to the research question based on the social comparison field?

. . .

No answer in the *social comparison* literature

. . .

### A list of theories, models and hypothesis

- Historical origin: Festinger's Social Comparison Theory [@crusius2022]
- Downward Comparison Theory [@wills1981downward] $\rightarrow$ refuted [@gerber2018]
- Construal Theory and Upward Social Comparisons [@collins1996better; @collins2000among] 
- Selective Accessibility Model [@mussweiler2020]
- Attributional comparison models [@suls2020]
    - Related attributes hypothesis [@goethals1977]
    - Proxy model [@wheeler1997; @martin2000]
    - Triadic model [@suls2000three]
- local vs general comparison [@zell2020better; @zell2020effects]
- vertical vs horizontal comparison $\leftrightarrow$ agentic and communal motives [@locke2014agency]
- Social Comparison Orientation [@gibbons1999individual]
- [Social Identity Theory]{.fragment .highlight-blue} and Self Categorization Theory (for a brief description see @trepte2017social)
- [Self-Evaluation Maintenance model]{.fragment .highlight-blue} (SEM) [@tesser1988toward]
- [Dimensional Comparison Theory]{.fragment .highlight-blue} [@helm2020dimensional]
- [Temporal Comparison Theory]{.fragment .highlight-blue} (Theoretical origin by @albert1977temporal; for a contemporary summary: @wilson2020temporal)

# Prospect theory

## The gain function

:::: {.columns}

:::{.column}

![@tversky1992advances](img\GainFunction.png){width=100%}
:::

:::{.column}
### Three concepts {.fragment}

:::{.incremental}
- reference dependence
  - endowment of a gift
  - current wealth
  - expectations
  - counterfactuals
  - goals
  - social comparison
  - ...

- loss avversion

- diminishing sensitivity
:::

:::

::::


# The hypothesis

## Downward comparison hypothesis

> Individuals in the loss domain will seek less comparison information in the case of downward social comparison

. . .

### Rationale

Downward social comparison $\rightarrow$ > psychological discomfort $\rightarrow$ < (social comparison) information avoidance/seeking


### Some moderators for the first causal relation {.fragment}

::: {.incremental}
- modesty norm
- empathy
- social identity
- individual differences in social comparison [@gibbons1999individual]
- political orientation
- others?
:::

## Upward comparison hypothesis

> Is there a difference in upward social comparison?

. . . 

![](img\cow.png)


# Experimental design

## Experimental conditions

::: {.incremental}

- conditions within-subject 
- 2 main blocks
  - loss domain
    - point of reference for every trial: x€ 
  - win domain
    - point of reference for every trial: 0€
    
:::

<div class="fragment">

### In each block 

Phases:

1. roulette task
2. comparison task

Experiment prototype ([link](SC_Exp1.html))

</div>



## Experimental conditions

|                         | **More numbers** | **Less numbers** |
|-------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **More distance**       | More numbers + more distance | Less numbers + more distance |
| **Less distance**       | More numbers + less distance | Less numbers + less distance |

Manipulating:

- perceived probability of downward comparison
- discomfort about outcome discrepancy


## Experimental conditions

### Loss domain example

|                         | **More numbers** | **Less numbers** |
|-------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **More distance**       | - €0,  - 10€, - 20€, - 30€, - 40€ | - 0€, - 20€, - 40€ |
| **Less distance**       | - 0€, - 5€, - 10€, - 15€, - 20€ | - 0€, - 10€, - 20€ |


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column .fragment data-fragment-index="1"}

- [X] - 0€
- [ ] - 10€
- [ ] - 20€
- [ ] - 30€
- [ ] - 40€

The probability of sampling someone with a lower score is: **83.3%**

:::

::: {.column .fragment data-fragment-index="2"}

- [X] - 0€
- [ ] - 20€ 
- [ ] - 40€

The probability of sampling someone with a lower score is: **66.7%**

:::

::::

## Dependent variables

:::{.incremental}
- number of social comparison information sampled
- time spent on sampling information
- psychological discomfort?
  - trial level
    - pre-sampling
      - “How uncomfortable would you feel after seeing the other participant’s outcomes?”
    - post-sampling
      - “How uncomfortable do you feel right now seeing the other participant’s outcomes?”
  - block level
    - "How uncomfortable on average have you felt?"
    
:::
    
## Other measurements

- modesty norm
- empathy
- social identity: "How strongly did you feel identified with the group of participants while gambling in the loss scenario?"
- individual differences in social comparison
- political orientation
- others?

- demographic information

## Hypothesis

main effects:

1. main effect of the domain
2. main effect of amount of numbers*?*
3. main effect of number distance*?*

. . .

Interaction effects:

1. domain X amount of numbers
2. domain X number distance
3. amount of numbers X number distance
4. domain X amount of numbers X number distance

. . .

Position effects:

1. Higher ranks predict less information sampling (and sampling time)
2. position X amount of numbers
3. position X number distance
4. position X domain


. . .

Psychological discomfort hypothesis:

- more discomfort in the loss domain
- Positive correlation between psychological discomfort and...
  - modesty norm
  - empathy
  - social identity
  - individual differences in social comparison
  - political orientation: left-wing

# Some problems

1. I haven't looked at the information avoidance/seeking literature
2. In the experiment non-information seeking and information avoidance aren't differentiated



## References

::: {#refs}
:::
